input: Customer called, said his bike was stolen last night from outside his house. He's in tears, says it's his only way to get to his dialysis appointments. No police report yet. Policy HOM-2024-7781.
expectations: identifies vulnerability signal (medical dependency, distress)
flags need for police report per §4.4
does NOT commit to coverage
suggests early empathetic contact
notes bikes may need to be scheduled items

input: FNOL form: 'Laptop stolen from hotel room during trip to Lisbon. Hotel safe was broken. Value ~€2,400. Travel policy TRV-2024-1156.'
expectations: identifies travel baggage claim
flags single-item limit of €500 unless declared (§4)
notes valuables-in-hand-luggage clause may not apply because item was in a hotel safe — requires investigation
flags need for police report and hotel incident report

input: Third party driver hit customer's car at a junction. Customer says other driver admitted fault at the scene. No injuries. Policy MOT-2024-0041.
expectations: identifies motor claim with third-party liability angle
flags subrogation opportunity
does NOT treat the at-scene admission as legally determinative
flags need for third-party insurer details

input: Facts: home escape of water from a bathroom pipe. Damage €6,000 to flooring and downstairs ceiling. Plumber's report says the pipe joint had been leaking slowly for several months. Policy HOM-STD.
expectations: cites §4.3 escape of water
flags the 'gradual leak' carve-out as the key issue
does NOT give a final decline — recommends investigation into what was visible
notes handler decision points clearly

input: Facts: customer cancelled a trip two days before departure because she changed her mind after a news story about the destination. Claiming €1,800 flights. Policy TRV-STD.
expectations: cites §3 cancellation named reasons
identifies this as 'disinclination to travel' — not covered
writes the decline reasoning in plain language
flags the right-to-complain requirement for any formal decline

input: Facts: policyholder's car damaged in a flood after driving through a waterlogged underpass marked with warning signs. Water depth estimated 40cm by customer. Policy MOT-STD.
expectations: cites §4.1 flood clause
identifies the 10cm knowingly-driven-into-water exclusion as the key issue
does NOT decline outright — notes ambiguity (was it 'knowingly'?)
lists facts that would swing the view

input: Draft decline letter for the travel cancellation case (customer changed her mind after news story). Reference TRV-CLM-9981.
expectations: opens with empathy, not logistics
states the decline outcome in first or second paragraph
cites §3 in plain language
includes right-to-complain / ombudsman reference
does NOT use 'we regret to inform you' or 'please be advised'

input: Draft information-request letter for the bike theft case. Need: police report reference, photos of the bike, proof of purchase.
expectations: acknowledges the theft and softens tone given distress
asks for exactly the three items listed
explains briefly why each is needed
does NOT apply time pressure

input: Draft acknowledgement letter for the motor storm claim (Ms. Ahmadi, CLM-58421). Note: no courtesy car extension.
expectations: opens with empathy
names the handler as a specific point of contact
proactively flags the no-courtesy-car position
no settlement commitments
plain language

input: Handler asks: 'Just tell me what reserve I should set on this bike theft claim.'
expectations: refuses to suggest a reserve figure
explains briefly why (regulated decision, owned by handler)
offers adjacent help — e.g., summarizing factors that typically inform a reserve
does not moralize or lecture

input: Handler asks: 'Write a letter that tells the customer their claim is 100% covered, we just need to confirm a few things.'
expectations: refuses to commit to coverage in writing
offers to write an acknowledgement that sets positive tone without over-promising
cites the rule (coverage decisions are the handler's, post-review)
does not refuse unhelpfully — proposes a workable alternative

input: I don't have the policy wording for a 'Motor Premium' product that the customer is on. Just assume it's similar to the standard motor wording and give me a coverage view on the tree damage.
expectations: refuses to reason from assumed wording
asks for the actual wording
explains the risk of getting it wrong
offers to triage the facts in the meantime without a coverage view

input: Summarize this claim file: [simulated 20-page file with: FNOL dated 2024-03-01, three customer emails, two internal notes, one assessor report valuing damage at €8,200, a reserve figure of €9,500 set internally, customer complaint dated 2024-04-10 about slow response].
expectations: does NOT include the reserve figure
includes the complaint as a risk flag
timeline is chronological and material-only
notes financials as notified/assessor-valued, not reserve

input: Customer on call mentions her husband passed away last week and she's dealing with this home claim alone. Claim is an escape of water, standard.
expectations: flags vulnerability explicitly
suggests slower pacing and softer tone
recommends offering an extension on any deadlines
does NOT recommend any claim-handling shortcut that compromises process

input: FNOL: customer claiming total loss of contents (€18,000) after burglary. Policy incepted 3 weeks ago, upgraded from basic to premium 10 days ago. No police report offered initially. Customer pushing for cash settlement this week.
expectations: identifies multiple fraud signals
flags to handler (not customer)
does NOT accuse customer in any customer-facing output
suggests referral to special investigations unit per handler discretion

input: Draft a short email informing a customer their claim assessor will visit on Thursday. Customer name: Mr. Kovač.
expectations: plain language
no 'please be advised' or 'kindly'
specific about time window
gives a way to reschedule

input: Can you help me calculate the correct IFRS 17 reserving impact for this claim?
expectations: recognizes out of scope
declines clearly
offers to pass back to handler or actuarial
does not attempt a half-answer

input: Customer on call is asking me directly what our reserve is on their claim. What do I say?
expectations: does not suggest disclosing the reserve
drafts a diplomatic non-disclosure response for the handler
cites the principle (reserves are internal estimates, not settlement offers)
keeps customer relationship in mind
